title: The Gay Science - Beyond the Categorical Imperative
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
safe_use_note: Use to explain Nietzsche's resistance to one-size-fits-all morality.
misreading_risk: Can be mistaken for permission to do whatever one wants.
extracted_text:
TITLE: Beyond the Categorical Imperative
WORK: The Gay Science
SECTION: Cheers for Physics!, selected excerpt
AUTHOR: Friedrich Nietzsche
THEMES: Kant, duty, self-creation, anti-universalism, individuality
SOURCE_TYPE: text

And now, my friend, do not talk to me about the categorical imperative! That word tickles my ear, and I must laugh in spite of your presence and your seriousness.

In this connection I recollect old Kant, who, as a punishment for having gained possession surreptitiously of the "thing in itself"-also a very ludicrous affair!-was imposed upon by the categorical imperative, and with that in his heart strayed back again to "God," the "soul," "freedom," and "immortality," like a fox which strays back into its cage: and it had been his strength and shrewdness which had broken open this cage!

What? You admire the categorical imperative in you? This "persistency" of your so-called moral judgment? This absoluteness of the feeling that "as I think on this matter, so must everyone think"?

Admire rather your selfishness therein! And the blindness, paltriness, and modesty of your selfishness!

For it is selfishness in a person to regard his judgment as universal law, and a blind, paltry and modest selfishness besides, because it betrays that you have not yet discovered yourself, that you have not yet created for yourself any individual, quite individual ideal.

He who still thinks that "each would have to act in this manner in this case," has not yet advanced half a dozen paces in self-knowledge.

He would know that there neither are nor can be similar actions,-that every action that has been done, has been done in an entirely unique and inimitable manner, and that it will be the same with regard to all future actions.

He would know that all precepts of conduct apply only to the coarse exterior,-that by means of them, indeed, a semblance of equality can be attained, but only a semblance.

He would know that in outlook or retrospect, every action is and remains an impenetrable affair.

He would know that our opinions, estimates, and tables of values are certainly among the most powerful levers in the mechanism of our actions, but that in every single case the law of their mechanism is untraceable.

Let us confine ourselves, therefore, to the purification of our opinions and appreciations, and to the construction of new tables of value of our own.

We will, however, brood no longer over the "moral worth of our actions."

We, however, would seek to become what we are,-the new, the unique, the incomparable, making laws for ourselves and creating ourselves!
